raw_text: Nelis observes that Lucan 1.33-66 (in praise of Nero) can only properly be understood in relation to Georg. 1.25-40, 489-92, and 505-11 (in praise of Augustus). Paolucci analyzes how the debate among European humanists over the Vergilian cento Medea , later attributed to Hosidius Geta, contributes to our understanding of the circulation of the codex Salmasianus (Paris, BNF, Lat. 10318) before it reached the hands of Claude Saumaise in 1615. Prenner discusses the many Vergilian verses inserted into the Historia Augusta. Salanitro feels that the use of Homeric and Vergilian texts in the production of centones in late antiquity merits greater scholarly attention than it has received. Spentzou writes that Silius' Punica attempts to subvert the primarily Vergilian epic code. Parallels with Aeneas make it apparent that Hannibal is the dominant figure, although Scipio is Aeneas' most convincing successor. Vergilius 58 (2012) 103 Shirley Werner Adorjáni, Zsolt. Nelis observes that Lucan 1.33-66 (in praise of Nero) can only properly
be understood in relation to Georg. 1.25-40, 489-92, and 505-11 (in praise of
Augustus). Paolucci analyzes how the debate among European humanists over
the Vergilian cento Medea , later attributed to Hosidius Geta, contributes to
our understanding of the circulation of the codex Salmasianus (Paris, BNF,
Lat. 10318) before it reached the hands of Claude Saumaise in 1615. Prenner
discusses the many Vergilian verses inserted into the Historia Augusta. Salanitro
feels that the use of Homeric and Vergilian texts in the production of centones in
late antiquity merits greater scholarly attention than it has received. Spentzou
writes that Silius' Punica attempts to subvert the primarily Vergilian epic code.
Parallels with Aeneas make it apparent that Hannibal is the dominant figure,
although Scipio is Aeneas' most convincing successor.
